questions.stem: The surgical team is about to make the first incision. Per the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist, which action must occur at this exact moment, and who must participate?

options.text: The surgeon alone verbally confirms the surgical site using the patient's consent form
The circulating nurse confirms patient identity and procedure using the patient's wristband
The entire surgical team pauses and verbally confirms: patient identity, surgical site and side, planned procedure, positioning, antibiotic prophylaxis, and any anticipated critical concerns
The anesthesiologist confirms ETT placement with waveform capnography before the time-out begins

questions.rationale: The WHO Surgical Safety Checklist 'time-out' occurs IMMEDIATELY before skin incision and is a mandatory complete pause involving ALL team members: surgeon, anesthesia provider, scrub technician, and circulating nurse. Each member must actively participate — passive listening does not satisfy the requirement. Items confirmed during time-out: (1) Patient identity (name, DOB, MRN); (2) Surgical site and laterality (confirmed with visible site marking); (3) Planned procedure; (4) Correct patient positioning; (5) Antibiotic prophylaxis administered within the prior 60 minutes; (6) Relevant imaging displayed; (7) Anticipated critical steps, significant blood loss, or special equipment needs. The time-out is distinct from the 'sign-in' (before anesthesia induction) and 'sign-out' (before leaving the OR). Implementation has reduced wrong-site surgery, wrong-patient surgery, and serious complications by more than 50% globally.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "The surgeon alone verbally confirms the surgical site using the patient's consent form" moves care toward routine care before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.b: "The circulating nurse confirms patient identity and procedure using the patient's wristband" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Per the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist, which action must occur at this exact moment" points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "The entire surgical team pauses and verbally confirms: patient identity, surgical site and side, planned procedure" fits the safer response to the cue "(7) Anticipated critical steps, significant blood loss, or special equipment needs".

questions.takeaway: Time-out: ALL team members pause immediately before incision to verbally confirm patient ID, site/side, procedure, positioning, antibiotic timing, and critical concerns. Individual confirmation is insufficient.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse finds a conscious patient with type 1 diabetes with a blood glucose of 54 mg/dL and symptoms of shakiness and diaphoresis. The patient is alert and able to swallow. Which is the CORRECT initial treatment?

options.text: Administer glucagon 1 mg subcutaneously
Administer 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrate orally (e.g., 4 oz juice or glucose tablets) and recheck glucose in 15 minutes
Administer 50 mL of 50% dextrose (D50) IV push
Give the patient a full meal immediately to restore blood glucose

questions.rationale: The '15-15 rule' is the standard for treating conscious, alert patients with mild-to-moderate hypoglycemia who can swallow safely: give 15 grams of rapid-acting carbohydrate (4 oz orange juice, 4 oz regular soda, or 3-4 glucose tablets), wait 15 minutes, and recheck blood glucose. If still < 70 mg/dL, repeat the treatment. This is the safest, most physiologically appropriate approach. Glucagon is reserved for unconscious or unable-to-swallow patients. D50 IV push is reserved for unconscious patients or those without IV access who cannot take oral carbohydrates. A full meal is slower to absorb and can cause a prolonged correction — it is the follow-up after glucose normalizes, not the acute treatment.

distractorRationales.a: "Administer glucagon 1 mg subcutaneously" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Reserve glucagon for unconscious/unable-to-swallow" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Administer 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrate orally (e.g., 4 oz juice or glucose tablets) and recheck" fits the safer response to the cue "Reserve D50 for severe hypoglycemia without oral access".
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Administer 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrate orally (e.g., 4 oz juice or glucose tablets) and recheck" fits the safer response to the cue "A nurse finds a conscious patient with type 1 diabetes with a blood glucose of 54 mg/dL".

questions.takeaway: Conscious hypoglycemic patient who can swallow: 15g fast-acting carbs (15-15 rule) → recheck in 15 min → repeat if still low → then a snack with protein. Reserve glucagon for unconscious/unable-to-swallow. Reserve D50 for severe hypoglycemia without oral access.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse receives a SBAR hand-off report. The oncoming nurse should be MOST concerned about which patient?

options.text: A 67-year-old with COPD whose O2 sat is 91% on 2L nasal cannula, which is their baseline
A 52-year-old with sepsis whose urine output has been 15 mL/hour for the past 3 hours
A 78-year-old post-hip replacement who refuses to ambulate and states 'I'm tired'
A 45-year-old on heparin infusion whose aPTT result is pending from 2 hours ago

questions.rationale: Urine output of 15 mL/hr for 3 consecutive hours (total 45 mL in 3 hours, target minimum 30 mL/hr) in a patient with sepsis signals inadequate renal perfusion and worsening organ dysfunction. This meets criteria for oliguria and may indicate septic shock progression or acute kidney injury, requiring immediate provider notification and likely fluid resuscitation. The COPD patient's 91% sat is their established baseline; the hip replacement refusal is important but not acutely threatening; the pending aPTT should be followed but is not immediately dangerous.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "In sepsis, urine output < 0" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "A 78-year-old post-hip replacement who refuses to ambulate and states 'I'm tired'" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "This meets criteria for oliguria and may indicate septic shock progression or acute kidney injury, requiring immediate provider" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "The COPD patient's 91% sat is their established baseline" suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so "A 45-year-old on heparin infusion whose aPTT result is pending from 2 hours ago" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: In sepsis, urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/hr for > 2 hours is an organ dysfunction criterion under Sepsis-3. Prioritize signs of end-organ perfusion failure over subjective complaints or pending results.

visualRationale.title: Safety and Infection Control / Prioritization
visualRationale.caption: In sepsis, urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/hr for > 2 hours is an organ dysfunction criterion under Sepsis-3. Prioritize signs of end-organ perfusion failure over subjective complaints or pending results.
visualRationale.conclusion: In sepsis, urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/hr for > 2 hours is an organ dysfunction criterion under Sepsis-3. Prioritize signs of end-organ perfusion failure over subjective complaints or pending results.

questions.stem: A clinic nurse suspects a patient has active tuberculosis based on symptoms and a positive QuantiFERON test. The patient has not yet been confirmed by sputum culture. What is the nurse's legal obligation regarding reporting?

options.text: Wait for confirmed sputum culture before reporting to the health department
Report only if the patient is homeless or a healthcare worker
Report the suspected case to the local public health department — TB is reportable on suspicion, not confirmation
The physician is solely responsible for reporting; nurses have no legal duty to report

questions.rationale: Mandatorily reportable diseases are reported on the basis of clinical suspicion, not confirmation. For TB and other high-priority communicable diseases, waiting for laboratory confirmation delays contact tracing and public health intervention. Reportable diseases vary by state but consistently include TB, STIs (gonorrhea, syphilis, chlamydia), meningococcal disease, hepatitis A/B/C, HIV, measles, pertussis, and foodborne illness outbreaks. Nurses have an independent legal duty to report regardless of physician action. The report goes to the local/state health department, which then initiates contact tracing.

distractorRationales.a: The cue "For TB and other high-priority communicable diseases, waiting for laboratory confirmation delays contact tracing and public health intervention" suggests clinical safety risk, so "Wait for confirmed sputum culture before reporting to the health department" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.b: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Report the suspected case to the local public health department — TB is reportable on suspicion" fits the safer response to the cue "For TB and other high-priority communicable diseases, waiting for laboratory confirmation delays contact tracing and public health intervention".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "The physician is solely responsible for reporting; nurses have no legal duty to report" moves care toward routine care before bedside safety action.

questions.takeaway: Mandatory reportable diseases: report on suspicion (not confirmation); TB, STIs, meningitis, hepatitis — nurse has independent duty to report to public health; do not wait for lab confirmation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient with a legally designated healthcare proxy (durable power of attorney for healthcare) is brought to the ED unconscious after a large stroke. The healthcare proxy, who has legal authority, requests that all life-sustaining treatment be withdrawn. No written advance directive exists. What is the nurse's most appropriate action?

options.text: Proceed with withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment as legally directed by the healthcare proxy
Disregard the proxy's decision and provide full resuscitation until the patient can speak for themselves
Consult the ethics committee and continue full treatment until the committee renders a decision
Contact adult protective services, as a family member withdrawing treatment may constitute elder abuse

questions.rationale: A legally designated healthcare proxy (durable power of attorney for healthcare) has the legal authority to make medical decisions on behalf of an incapacitated patient. The proxy's role is substituted judgment — making the decision the patient would make if able. When a legally designated proxy with proper authority requests withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment, this request must be honored following institutional policies and applicable state law. The nurse should: verify the proxy's legal authority, document the decision, notify the physician immediately, involve the palliative care team, and provide comfort measures. An ethics consultation may be helpful if there is team conflict, but it is not required to delay a legally sound decision. This is not elder abuse — it is the proper exercise of legally granted decision-making authority.

distractorRationales.b: "Disregard the proxy's decision and provide full resuscitation until the patient can speak for themselves" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The nurse should: verify the proxy's legal authority, document the decision, notify the physician immediately, involve the palliative" points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "The nurse should: verify the proxy's legal authority, document the decision, notify the physician immediately, involve the palliative" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Contact adult protective services, as a family member withdrawing treatment may constitute elder abuse" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The nurse should: verify the proxy's legal authority, document the decision, notify the physician immediately, involve the palliative" points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.

questions.takeaway: Healthcare proxy with legal authority speaks for the incapacitated patient. Their decision must be respected and acted upon. No written advance directive is required when a valid proxy is designated.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An RN delegates to an LPN/LVN. Which task is within the LPN's scope of practice in most states?

options.text: Perform the initial nursing admission assessment for a newly admitted patient
Administer oral, IM, and subcutaneous medications and monitor response
Develop and update the nursing care plan based on patient assessment findings
Interpret cardiac rhythm strips and adjust IV vasopressor infusions per protocol

questions.rationale: LPN/LVN scope of practice typically includes: administering medications (oral, IM, SC, topical), performing basic wound care, collecting specimens, and monitoring and reporting patient status. Initial assessments (A) require RN-level nursing judgment and are an RN responsibility. Care plan development (C) is an RN function. Interpreting arrhythmias and adjusting vasopressors (D) requires advanced critical care RN knowledge. The key distinction is that LPNs implement care under RN supervision; RNs assess, plan, evaluate, and teach.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Administer oral, IM, and subcutaneous medications and monitor response" fits the safer response to the cue "Interpreting arrhythmias and adjusting vasopressors (D) requires advanced critical care RN knowledge".
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Develop and update the nursing care plan based on patient assessment findings" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Interpret cardiac rhythm strips and adjust IV vasopressor infusions per protocol" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.

questions.takeaway: LPN scope: administer medications, perform basic wound care, collect data, implement established care — initial assessment, care planning, and teaching are RN responsibilities.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient with severe asthma is admitted with an acute exacerbation. The nurse notes the patient is no longer wheezing despite having difficulty breathing and accessory muscle use. Which interpretation is CORRECT?

options.text: The patient is improving — decreased wheezing indicates reduced bronchospasm
The patient is deteriorating — absence of wheezing with severe work of breathing indicates 'silent chest,' signaling impending respiratory failure
The patient requires a bronchodilator since the wheezing has resolved
The patient is having an allergic reaction rather than an asthma attack

questions.rationale: A 'silent chest' in an asthmatic patient with severe respiratory distress is an ominous sign — it does not indicate improvement. It indicates airways are so severely obstructed that no air movement is occurring, thus no wheeze can be generated. This is a pre-arrest condition requiring immediate escalation: notify provider, prepare for intubation, consider IV magnesium sulfate, IV epinephrine or terbutaline, and high-dose continuous bronchodilators. The paradox of asthma: the sicker the patient, the less they wheeze because they lack airflow. Wheezing returning with treatment is actually a sign of improvement.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "high-dose continuous bronchodilators" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "The patient is deteriorating — absence of wheezing with severe work of breathing indicates 'silent chest,'" fits the safer response to the cue "The paradox of asthma: the sicker the patient, the less they wheeze because they lack airflow".
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "high-dose continuous bronchodilators" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Silent chest in asthma = no airflow = impending respiratory arrest. This is a critical sign, NOT improvement. Escalate immediately. Wheezing on treatment = airways opening = improvement.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Asthma Exacerbation
visualRationale.caption: Silent chest in asthma = no airflow = impending respiratory arrest. This is a critical sign, NOT improvement. Escalate immediately. Wheezing on treatment = airways opening = improvement.
visualRationale.conclusion: Silent chest in asthma = no airflow = impending respiratory arrest. This is a critical sign, NOT improvement. Escalate immediately. Wheezing on treatment = airways opening = improvement.

questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: Parents call the pediatric triage nurse stating their 2-year-old has been having a seizure for 1 minute. The child has a temperature of 39.2°C (102.6°F) and no prior seizure history. What is the most important teaching point the nurse should emphasize?

options.text: Administer rectal diazepam immediately regardless of seizure duration
Most simple febrile seizures resolve within 5 minutes; call 911 if the seizure lasts more than 5 minutes or the child stops breathing
Put fingers in the mouth to prevent tongue-swallowing during the seizure
Administer acetaminophen immediately to stop the seizure

questions.rationale: Simple febrile seizures are benign, typically last <5 minutes, and are self-limiting. Parent teaching: (1) Do not restrain the child; (2) Turn child on side to prevent aspiration; (3) Do not insert anything in the mouth — risk of injury, not tongue-swallowing; (4) Time the seizure; (5) Call 911 if seizure >5 minutes (status epilepticus risk), child stops breathing, or a second seizure occurs. Rectal diazepam is not administered routinely unless prescribed for a child with known prolonged febrile seizures. Antipyretics do not abort an ongoing seizure.

distractorRationales.a: "Administer rectal diazepam immediately regardless of seizure duration" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Febrile seizure parent teaching: most resolve in <5 min" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Teaching items should reinforce the specific medication, symptom, or safety rule rather than inventing a new emergency.
distractorRationales.c: "Put fingers in the mouth to prevent tongue-swallowing during the seizure" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "6°F) and no prior seizure history" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so patient teaching and medication-safety reinforcement is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Febrile seizure parent teaching: most resolve in <5 min" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Administer acetaminophen immediately to stop the seizure" would not correct the active problem. Teaching items should reinforce the specific medication, symptom, or safety rule rather than inventing a new emergency.

questions.takeaway: Febrile seizure parent teaching: most resolve in <5 min; position on side, do not restrain, do not put anything in mouth; call 911 if >5 min or breathing stops.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Febrile seizure parent teaching: most resolve in <5 min; position on side, do not restrain, do not put anything in mouth; call 911 if >5 min or breathing stops.
visualRationale.conclusion: Febrile seizure parent teaching: most resolve in <5 min; position on side, do not restrain, do not put anything in mouth; call 911 if >5 min or breathing stops.

questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient is being discharged on prednisone 40 mg daily for 10 days following an acute COPD exacerbation. Which statement by the patient indicates that ADDITIONAL teaching is needed?

options.text: 'I should not stop this medication suddenly without talking to my doctor.'
'I can stop taking this medication early if I start feeling better.'
'I should monitor my blood sugar more closely while taking this medication.'
'I should take this medication in the morning with food.'

questions.rationale: Stopping corticosteroids abruptly or early is dangerous and incorrect. Even a 10-day course suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and requires a tapering strategy if used longer, and should always be completed as prescribed for a short course. Early discontinuation risks adrenal insufficiency and relapse of the condition being treated. The patient should understand that the full course must be completed, and that stopping suddenly (in longer courses) can cause an adrenal crisis. Statement A is correct — do not stop without medical advice. Statement C is correct — steroids cause hyperglycemia. Statement D is correct — morning administration mimics cortisol's natural diurnal rhythm and reduces GI upset.

distractorRationales.a: "'I should not stop this medication suddenly without talking to my doctor.'" is accurate teaching, so it would not show the misunderstanding asked for in the stem. Do not stop without medical advice.
distractorRationales.c: "'I should monitor my blood sugar more closely while taking this medication.'" is accurate teaching, so it would not show the misunderstanding asked for in the stem. Steroids cause hyperglycemia.
distractorRationales.d: "'I should take this medication in the morning with food.'" is accurate teaching, so it would not show the misunderstanding asked for in the stem. Morning administration mimics cortisol's natural diurnal rhythm and reduces GI upset.

questions.takeaway: Corticosteroid teaching: complete the full course, do not stop early or abruptly. Monitor blood glucose. Take in morning with food. Know signs of infection (immunosuppression). Report mood changes, edema, GI symptoms.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient on warfarin therapy for atrial fibrillation has an INR of 4.8 (therapeutic range 2.0-3.0). The patient has no active bleeding. What is the MOST appropriate nursing action?

options.text: Administer vitamin K 10 mg IV push immediately
Hold the warfarin dose, notify the provider, and monitor closely for bleeding signs
Administer fresh frozen plasma to rapidly reverse anticoagulation
Continue warfarin at the same dose and recheck INR in 3 days

questions.rationale: An INR of 4.8 represents supratherapeutic anticoagulation with elevated bleeding risk, but no active bleeding is present. Standard management for INR 4-10 without bleeding in a patient on warfarin for non-mechanical valve indication: hold warfarin, monitor for bleeding signs, and the INR will self-correct over 24-72 hours as the drug is metabolized. The provider should be notified to evaluate for dose adjustment. Vitamin K is indicated for INR > 10, or INR 4-10 with bleeding risk factors or active bleeding. High-dose IV vitamin K (10 mg) causes prolonged warfarin resistance and is reserved for emergencies. FFP is for life-threatening bleeding requiring immediate reversal. Continuing the same dose ignores the supratherapeutic level.

distractorRationales.a: "Administer vitamin K 10 mg IV push immediately" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Supratherapeutic INR (no bleeding): INR 4-10 → hold warfarin + notify provider + monitor" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "INR > 10 or any bleeding → vitamin K" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "High-dose IV vitamin K (10 mg) causes prolonged warfarin resistance and is reserved for emergencies" suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so "Continue warfarin at the same dose and recheck INR in 3 days" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Supratherapeutic INR (no bleeding): INR 4-10 → hold warfarin + notify provider + monitor. INR > 10 or any bleeding → vitamin K. Life-threatening bleeding → FFP or 4-factor PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate). Never continue the same dose if INR is out of range.
